title: Benin 2025 Coup Attempt
content: Benin 2025
A small military faction calling itself the Comité Militaire pour la Refondation (CMR) failed to depose President Patrice Talon after a brief armed assault on the presidency.

Benin, once a bastion of democratic stability, faced a polarised political scene and authoritarian drift under President Talon. The move to create a second legislative chamber (Senate) and extend presidential terms stoked opposition fears of a permanent power grab. Simultaneously, the security environment in the north deteriorated significantly; 54 soldiers were killed in jihadist attacks in April 2025, leading to military grievances over negligence towards soldiers killed on the front and their families.

Early on December 7, gunfire erupted near the presidential residence in Cotonou. Eight-armed soldiers appeared on national television, led by Lieutenant-Colonel Pascal Tigri, claiming they had deposed the government to address the security failures against Al-Qaeda-linked groups. The attempt was quickly neutralized by loyalist forces with support from French intelligence and special forces. By the end of the day, the African Union and International IDEA issued strong condemnations, labeling the event a grave violation of constitutional order.

The failed putsch prompted a regional contagion alert, leading ECOWAS to consider military intervention to preserve civilian rule. President Talon utilized the aftermath to consolidate control, though international bodies urged him to respect term limits ahead of the 2026 elections. Analysts noted a possible AES influence, as Niger’s junta had reportedly been informed of the operation in advance.
references: Mesa, Aris. "Countering the Coup in Benin: A Jus ad Bellum Perspective." EJIL: Talk! (2025).
IRIS France. "Benin: What Does the 7 December Coup Attempt Reveal?" (2025).

title: Madagascar 2025 Coup Attempt
content: An elite unit known as CAPSAT overthrew President Andry Rajoelina following weeks of youth-led "Gen Z" protests over economic mismanagement and power shortages.

Madagascar suffered from extreme poverty, with over 75% of its 30 million people living below the poverty line. Public anger boiled over in September 2025 due to chronic electricity and water cuts. President Rajoelina’s attempts to bypass the legislature and dissolve the National Assembly to block an impeachment vote served as the final catalyst for the military’s intervention.

On October 12, the Army Personnel and Services Administration Corps (CAPSAT) seized the capital, Antananarivo, meeting little resistance as civilian police joined the demonstrators. The military unit’s leadership framed their action as answering the people’s calls. Rajoelina reportedly fled to Réunion island on a French military aircraft as the National Assembly proceeded to formally impeach him.

The African Union immediately suspended Madagascar’s membership, while the United Nations condemned the unconstitutional change of power. CAPSAT announced a two-year transitional period under military leadership. Domestically, the Malagasy ariary plummeted, and fuel imports were disrupted, plunging the nation into severe economic uncertainty.
references: Robert Lansing Institute. "October 2025 Coup in Madagascar: Causes and Consequences." 2025.

title: Bolivia 2024 Coup Attempt
content: On June 26, 2024, a faction of the Bolivian Army led by General Juan José Zúñiga attempted to seize power by occupying the government headquarters in La Paz before retreating hours later due to a lack of institutional support.

Bolivia suffered from a severe economic crisis characterized by dwindling foreign reserves, fuel shortages, and high inflation. Simultaneously, the ruling Movement Towards Socialism (MAS) party split into two rival factions led by President Luis Arce and former President Evo Morales, who were on a collision course for the 2025 presidential candidacy.

The military uprising targeted President Arce’s democratically elected government. In the mid-afternoon of June 26, 2024, soldiers and armored vehicles led by General Zúñiga occupied Plaza Murillo, the site of the country’s main legislative and executive offices.

An armored vehicle rammed into the main gates of the Palacio Quemado, allowing troops to breach the building. Within the palace hallway, President Arce personally confronted Zúñiga, ordering the general to demobilize his forces and withdraw. Zúñiga claimed the military intended to "restore democracy" and release political prisoners like Jeanine Áñez. The situation reached a turning point when Arce appointed a new military high command, including General José Wilson Sánchez as the new army chief. Sánchez immediately ordered all mobilized personnel to return to their units. Lacking broader military backing and facing civilian mobilization in defense of democracy, the rebellious troops began their retreat from the plaza roughly three hours after the occupation began.

Authorities later arrested Zúñiga and former Navy commander Juan Arnez Salvador on charges of terrorism and armed uprising. The event intensified national polarization, with detractors alleging a self-coup (autogolpe) intended to bolster Arce’s falling popularity, while the administration maintained it was a direct assault on constitutional order.
references: BBC News Mundo. "Bolivia: cómo fue el intento de golpe de Estado que denunció el presidente Arce." June 26, 2024.
Inter-American Commission on Human Rights. "IACHR Condemns Coup Attempt in Bolivia." July 3, 2024.

Mera Medina, María José. "The Aftermath of Bolivia's Failed Coup Attempt." October 9, 2024.
Scotland, Jennifer. "Divided Opinions After Failed Coup Underscore Bolivia's Political Fragility." RUSI, August 6, 2024.

title: Gabon 2023 Coup Attempt
content: On August 30, 2023, senior military officers in Gabon seized power, placing President Ali Bongo Ondimba under house arrest minutes after he declared victory in a disputed general election.

The coup ended the 56-year dynastic rule of the Bongo family, which was plagued by allegations of systemic corruption, election fraud, and economic inequality. Despite Gabon's status as an upper middle-income oil producer, approximately one-third of the population lived in poverty with high youth unemployment.

The primary target of the military intervention was President Ali Bongo Ondimba. Following the electoral authority's announcement of a victory for Bongo with 64.27% of the vote—results many observers deemed implausible—the military moved to annul the process. A group of high-ranking personnel, identifying as the Committee for the Transition and Restoration of Institutions (CTRI), appeared on national television to declare they were seizing power to defend peace and end the current regime. The decisive force was the Republican Guard, led by General Brice Oligui Nguema, Bongo’s cousin and former security chief.

The coup was largely nonviolent and received broad public support, with citizens celebrating in the streets of Libreville and Port-Gentil. Military units dissolved all state institutions, including the government, Senate, National Assembly, and Constitutional Court, and briefly closed the country's borders to consolidate control.

General Nguema was named transitional president, promising a return to civilian rule by 2025 while simultaneously consolidating power through a new constitution. Reports from 2025, however, note a landslide victory for Nguema, which some classify as a repackaging of military rule. Ali Bongo was released from house arrest into retirement, though his family faced detention for high treason. The country remains suspended from the African Union.
references: Beaumont, Peter. "Gabon Military Officers Declare Coup After Ali Bongo Wins Disputed Election." The Guardian, August 30, 2023.
Gaulme, François, and Paul Melly. "Gabon: Has an Almost-Exemplary Transition Produced a New Political Model?" Ifri Editorials, October 1, 2025.

title: Niger 2023 Coup Attempt
content: On July 26, 2023, members of Niger’s presidential guard led by General Abdourahamane Tchiani detained President Mohamed Bazoum in the capital and seized executive power, citing deteriorating national security and governance.

Niger had recently achieved its first peaceful, democratic transfer of power in 2021; however, the country faced chronic challenges including systemic poverty, corruption, and a surge in jihadist violence across the Sahel. These grievances, combined with rumored internal power struggles between Bazoum and military leadership, provided a pretext for the intervention.

The primary target of the coup was President Mohamed Bazoum, who was blockaded and detained within his palace in Niamey by his own Presidential Guard. Initially, the uprising appeared to be a localized mutiny, and the broader military, the Nigerien Armed Forces (FAN), signaled readiness to defend the constitutional order. However, the situation reached a turning point when the military high command, led by General Abdou Sidikou Issa, declared their support for the mutineers to avoid a deadly confrontation between units. On state television, Colonel-Major Amadou Abdramane announced the formation of the National Council for the Safeguard of the Homeland (CNSP). This announcement confirmed the dissolution of the constitution and the suspension of all government agencies. Key individuals involved in the consolidation of power included General Tchiani and General Moussa Salaou Barmou, a U.S.-trained special forces commander. Civilian protests in support of Bazoum were dispersed by gunfire from the Presidential Guard before the junta established full territorial control.

The coup resulted in the indefinite detention of President Bazoum and the imposition of severe ECOWAS regional sanctions that devastated Niger’s economy. Politically, the junta shifted away from Western security partnerships, expelling French and U.S. troops in favor of military cooperation with Russia and fellow juntas in Mali and Burkina Faso.
references: Ajala, Olayinka. "What Caused the Coup in Niger? An Expert Outlines Three Driving Factors." The Conversation, July 31, 2023.
Al Jazeera. "Niger military coup: What you need to know." July 27, 2023.

title: Sudan 2023 Coup Attempt
content: The 2023 conflict in Sudan began as a violent struggle for national control between two rival generals whose former alliance collapsed into a catastrophic civil war.

Following the 2019 ousting of dictator Omar al-Bashir, a fragile power-sharing agreement between military and civilian leaders began to unravel. A military coup in October 2021 further stymied democratic progress. Tensions then escalated over the timeline for integrating paramilitary forces into the national army.

On April 15, 2023, fierce fighting erupted in Khartoum and quickly spread across the country. The conflict pitted the Sudanese Armed Forces (SAF), led by General Abdel Fattah al-Burhan, against the paramilitary Rapid Support Forces (RSF), commanded by Mohamed Hamdan Dagalo, also known as Hemedti. This was not a typical coup against a civilian government but a struggle within the security sector itself, as the RSF sought to seize strategic sites including the airport and Presidential Palace. The target of the RSF’s initial offensive was the SAF's military command and the de facto leadership of al-Burhan. Neither side achieved a quick victory, as the SAF responded with airstrikes while the RSF leveraged its urban mobility. The failure to reach a rapid outcome transitioned the event from a power grab into a protracted civil war.

The war has created the world's largest displacement crisis, with over 13 million people forced from their homes. It has decimated the economy and caused widespread famine. Politically, the country remains fractured, with the SAF governing from Port Sudan while the RSF controls significant territory in the west and south.
references: Council on Foreign Relations. "Civil War in Sudan." Global Conflict Tracker. Updated December 16, 2025.
Kiros, Kidane. "The Ongoing War in Sudan and Its Implications for the Security and Stability of the Horn of Africa and Beyond." Policy Center for the New South, October 2024.

title: Sao Tome and Principe 2022 Coup Attempt
sourceLinkLabel: 2022 Sao Tome and Principe Coup Attempt
content: The 2022 coup attempt in Sao Tome and Principe was a sudden overnight raid on military barracks that shattered the peace of one of Africa’s most stable democracies.

Sao Tome and Principe is a multiparty democracy with a history of five failed coup attempts since 1991. In September 2022, the Independent Democratic Action party won legislative elections, leading to a peaceful transfer of power. Prime Minister Patrice Trovoada was sworn in just weeks before the attack.

Shortly after midnight on November 25, 2022, a small group of gunmen launched an attack on the national army headquarters in the capital city. The target of this coup attempt was the newly established government of Prime Minister Patrice Trovoada. Four civilians entered the barracks in search of weapons and abducted a sentry as a hostage. The group was led by Arlécio Costa, a former mercenary who had previously attempted a coup in 2003. Costa was reportedly disgruntled over the seizure of land by the previous administration. The military responded quickly, engaging in a battle that lasted until approximately 6:00 a.m. During the fighting, the army captured three of the intruders at the base. Authorities also arrested Delfim Neves, the former president of the National Assembly, on suspicion of supporting the plot. By dawn, the military had successfully thwarted the attempt and neutralized the attackers.

The event ended in tragedy when four suspects, including Arlécio Costa, died from torture while in military custody. While the government remains in power, the incident damaged the nation's democratic reputation. Several soldiers faced charges for the custodial deaths, and the army chief subsequently resigned.
references: Associated Press. "Prime Minister: Coup Thwarted in Sao Tome and Principe." November 25, 2022.
European Union External Action Service. "Sao Tome and Principe: Statement on the Coup Attempt." November 29, 2022.

title: Burkina Faso 2022 Coup Attempt
content: On September 30, 2022, Captain Ibrahim Traoré, a 34-year-old artillery officer frustrated with military leadership, led a coup that ousted Lieutenant Colonel Paul-Henri Sandaogo Damiba, the junta leader who had himself seized power in January 2022, consolidating military control over the transitional government.

Burkina Faso's political crisis intensified following the January 23–24, 2022, military coup that ousted President Roch Marc Christian Kaboré. Damiba, promising to restore security against a worsening Islamist insurgency, was initially welcomed by Burkinabe society frustrated by the civilian government's inability to contain jihadist violence. However, by September 2022, the security situation had deteriorated dramatically. Islamist groups controlled nearly 40 percent of Burkina Faso's territory; violence had surged, and military officers and frontline troops under Damiba's command complained that he prioritized his own political interests over defeating insurgents.

On September 30, Traoré and disaffected military officers arrested Damiba and announced his deposition via state television, citing his failure to address the country's deteriorating security situation. They suspended the constitution, dissolved the transitional government and legislature, and imposed a nationwide curfew from 9 pm to 5 am. Traoré declared himself head of state and head of the Patriotic Movement for Safeguard and Restoration. Initially, Damiba attempted to flee toward the French military base at Camp Kamboinsin to potentially mount a counter-coup; however, this claim is disputed by Traoré. On October 2, religious and community leaders mediated Damiba's formal resignation, and he fled to exile in Togo after securing guarantees including protection for his allies and respect for the ECOWAS transition timeline.

The international community condemned the coup immediately. ECOWAS, the African Union, the United Nations, France, and the United States all protested the unconstitutional seizure of power. However, Traoré promised to honor Damiba's ECOWAS commitment to restore civilian rule by July 2024. The coup set Burkina Faso on a trajectory of deepening military autocracy, expanding human rights abuses, and increasing isolation from international partners, reversing what remained of the country's democratic transition.
references: Africa Center for Strategic Studies. "Understanding Burkina Faso's Latest Coup." September 30, 2022.
Al Jazeera. "Ibrahim Traore Declared Burkina Faso President After Coup." October 6, 2022.

title: Burkina Faso 2022 Coup Attempt
content: On January 24, 2022, Lieutenant Colonel Paul-Henri Sandaogo Damiba led a military coup that ousted democratically elected President Roch Marc Christian Kaboré, citing the government's failure to contain a worsening Islamic insurgency.

Burkina Faso's transition to democracy following the 2014 popular uprising that ended Blaise Compaoré's 27-year rule proved fragile. Though President Kaboré won peaceful elections in 2015 and again in 2020, rising jihadist violence that began in 2015 and killed thousands destabilized the country. By late 2021, widespread public dissatisfaction mounted as the military and civilian population suffered repeated defeats against al-Qaeda and Islamic State-affiliated groups, with the November 2021 Inata attack killing 49 soldiers. Kaboré's December 2021 military reshuffle and the prime minister's resignation came too late to restore confidence.

On January 23–24, gunfire erupted at the presidential residence and military barracks in Ouagadougou. Soldiers from multiple garrisons participated, coordinating attacks that overwhelmed state security. Military units detained President Kaboré at the Sangoulé Lamizana barracks. By the afternoon of January 24, Captain Sidsore Kader Ouedraogo announced on state television that the Patriotic Movement for Safeguard and Restoration (MPSR), led by Lieutenant Colonel Paul-Henri Damiba, had seized power. The 41-year-old Damiba, recently promoted to command the capital's third military region, had military training in Paris and authored a book on West African counterterrorism. The coup dissolved parliament, suspended the constitution, and imposed a nationwide curfew. In military custody, Kaboré signed a resignation letter, and soldiers declared a promised return to civilian rule within a reasonable time frame.

Damiba initially received public support and limited international engagement, though ECOWAS and the African Union condemned the coup and demanded constitutional restoration. However, Damiba's inability to improve security—violence increased 23 percent during his eight months in office—led to his own overthrow by Captain Ibrahim Traoré on September 30, 2022. The January coup thus marked Burkina Faso's continued instability and military dominance over democratic governance.
references: Chin, John, Haleigh Bartos, and Aleksaundra Handrinos. "What Burkina Faso’s Tragic History Teaches Us." Journal of Democracy, October 2024.
Heo, Sora. "Vicious Cycles: Burkina Faso’s Coup Quandary." International Relations Review, January 3, 2023.
Human Rights Watch. "Burkina Faso: Coup Puts Rights at Risk." January 27, 2022.
United Nations. "Secretary-General Following Events in Burkina Faso with Deep Concern." January 24, 2022.

title: Sudan 2021 Coup Attempt
content: On October 25, 2021, General Abdel Fattah al-Burhan, chairman of Sudan's Sovereignty Council, orchestrated a military coup that dissolved the transitional civilian-led government and detained Prime Minister Abdalla Hamdok, reversing Sudan's democratic transition just months before the military was scheduled to transfer power to civilian leadership.

Sudan's August 2019 power-sharing arrangement between military and civilian leaders proved unsustainable. Under a 39-month transitional framework, the military would govern the first 21 months before transferring leadership of the Sovereignty Council to civilian representatives in November 2021. However, al-Burhan and military officers resisted civilian-led security sector reform, international pressure to refer ousted dictator Omar al-Bashir to the International Criminal Court, and investigations into military responsibility for the 2019 Khartoum massacre. Economic deterioration, inflation reaching approximately 200 percent, unemployment, and food insecurity created widespread public discontent that Prime Minister Hamdok's civilian government could not adequately address. The failed September 21 coup attempt by al-Bashir loyalists provided a pretext for military intervention.

On October 25, military forces arrested Hamdok and cabinet members, dissolved the Sovereignty Council and transitional government, suspended constitutional provisions granting civilians power, and declared a state of emergency. Al-Burhan justified the coup as necessary to prevent civil war and preserve national unity. Hamdok, initially held at al-Burhan's residence, was later placed under house arrest. Hundreds of thousands of pro-democracy protesters took to the streets of Khartoum, and security forces responded with violence, killing at least eight people and injuring over 140 demonstrators.

The African Union suspended Sudan's membership, while ECOWAS, the United Nations, the United States, and the European Union condemned the coup and withheld aid. On November 21, 2021, after international mediation, Hamdok and al-Burhan signed a power-sharing agreement that temporarily reinstated Hamdok as prime minister. However, his resignation in January 2022 ultimately consolidated military rule.
references: Arab Center Washington DC. "Sudan Coup: The Regional Interference behind a Faltering Transition." December 11, 2021.
Middle East Policy Council. "Sudanese Government Instability and Military Coup." March 20, 2024.
CNN. "Sudan coup explained: The military has taken over in Sudan." October 26, 2021.
Middle East Eye. "Sudan coup 2021: Who is Abdel Fattah al-Burhan?" October 26, 2021.
Human Rights Watch. "Sudan: Military Takeover Threatens Rights." October 26, 2021.

title: Sudan 2021 Coup Attempt
content: On September 21, 2021, al-Bashir loyalists and military officers from Sudan's armored divisions staged a failed coup attempt against the Sovereignty Council led by General Abdel Fattah al-Burhan, seeking to reverse Sudan's 2019 democratic transition and prevent their former leader's extradition to the International Criminal Court.

Sudan's political transition following the 2019 overthrow of long-time dictator Omar al-Bashir proved deeply fragile. The August 2019 power-sharing arrangement established a Sovereignty Council composed of military and civilian members, with al-Burhan as chair, while economist Abdalla Hamdok led a civilian transitional government. However, the military resisted civilian-led security sector reform, ICC referrals of al-Bashir, and investigations into the 2019 Khartoum massacre that implicated officers in serious abuses. Economic deterioration, inflation, and government ineffectiveness further strained the tenuous civil-military partnership.

On September 21, dawn attacks began when Major General Abdel-Baqi Bakrawi led officers and soldiers from the armored divisions in an attempt to seize the Sovereignty Council, military headquarters, and state television in Omdurman. Loyal military forces repelled the plotters, who eventually surrendered inside a weapons depot. By the end of the day, more than 40 military and civilian supporters of al-Bashir had been arrested. Prime Minister Hamdok condemned the orchestrated coup attempt as an extension of al-Bashir's legacy of undermining democratic transition, while the United Nations warned the event could derail Sudan's political progress.

The failed September 21 coup has been described by analysts as a trial balloon, revealing vulnerabilities in the transition that General al-Burhan would exploit just 34 days later during his successful October 25, 2021 coup, which dissolved the civilian government and reversed many of the democratic gains achieved after 2019.
references: Arab Center Washington DC. "Sudan Coup: The Regional Interference behind a Faltering Transition." December 11, 2021.
Voice of America. "Sudan Blames Failed Coup Attempt on Bashir Loyalists." September 21, 2021.
Al Jazeera. "Sudan's PM says coup plotters were military and non-military." September 21, 2021.
Middle East Policy Council. "Sudanese Government Instability and Military Coup." March 20, 2024.

title: Guinea 2021 Coup Attempt
content: Military special forces seized power in Guinea on September 5, 2021, arresting President Alpha Condé and dissolving the nation's democratic institutions.

Alpha Condé became Guinea's first democratically elected president in 2010 but grew increasingly authoritarian. In 2020, he oversaw a controversial constitutional amendment to bypass term limits. His third-term re-election was marred by violent state repression, widespread poverty, and allegations of government corruption.

On the morning of September 5, 2021, an elite military unit known as the Special Forces Group launched an assault in the capital city of Conakry. Led by Colonel Mamadi Doumbouya, a former French legionnaire, the unit targeted the Presidential Palace in the Kaloum neighborhood. Heavy gunfire broke out as the special forces moved to arrest President Alpha Condé. During the brief but intense combat, between eight and twenty military personnel were killed. The special forces successfully detained Condé, later circulating images of the barefoot and disheveled leader in their custody. Doumbouya appeared on state television draped in a national flag to announce the takeover, stating the military had seized power to end political personalization and address economic mismanagement.

Colonel Doumbouya was sworn in as interim president and established the National Committee for Reunification and Development to oversee the government. President Condé remained under house arrest without charge. International bodies including ECOWAS and the African Union suspended Guinea's membership and demanded a return to civilian rule.
references: Akuamoah, Ernest Mensah. "The Coup in Guinea: Causes and Consequences." Australian Institute of International Affairs, December 28, 2021.
BBC News. "Guinea Coup: Who Is Col Mamady Doumbouya?" October 1, 2021.

Freedom House. "Guinea: Freedom in the World 2022 Country Report." 2022.
U.S. Department of State. "2021 Country Reports on Human Rights Practices: Guinea." 2022.

title: Mali 2021 Coup Attempt
content: On May 24, 2021, Colonel Assimi Goïta, Mali's vice president and leader of a military coup nine months prior, orchestrated a second coup by arresting interim President Bah Ndaw and Prime Minister Moctar Ouane, effectively consolidating military control over the transitional government.

Mali's political crisis intensified following the August 18, 2020 military coup that ousted President Ibrahim Boubacar Keïta. International pressure from ECOWAS and the African Union forced the junta to establish a civilian-led transitional government, with Ndaw inaugurated as interim president on September 25, 2020, and Goïta as vice president. However, this civilian arrangement masked the military's continued dominance over state affairs.

On May 24, 2021, the interim government announced a cabinet reshuffle that removed two coup leaders—Colonel Sadio Camara (Defense Minister) and Colonel Modibo Kone (Security Minister)—from ministerial positions without consulting Goïta. That evening, military forces arrested both Ndaw and Ouane and detained them at the Soundiata Keïta military camp in Kati. On May 25, Goïta announced the coup on national television, claiming the leaders had violated the transition charter and attempted to sabotage the process. Both leaders resigned under detention on May 26 and were released on May 27. Mali's Constitutional Court subsequently declared Goïta interim president, and he was sworn in on June 7. Choguel Kokalla Maïga, from the M5-RFP opposition coalition, was appointed prime minister in June.

The international community condemned the coup immediately. ECOWAS suspended Mali and imposed sanctions on transitional leaders, while the African Union, United Nations, France, and the United States demanded a return to democratic governance. Mali's election timeline collapsed; instead of elections in February 2022, the junta extended the transition to 2026, effectively entrenching military rule.
references: Akinola, Adeoye O., and Ratidzo Makombe. "Rethinking the Resurgence of Military Coups in Africa." SAGE Open, 2025.
Cold-Ravnkilde, Signe Marie, and Almamy Sylla. "The rise of Mali's videomen as cybercombatants." International Affairs, 2024.

title: Chad 2021 Coup Attempt
content: The sudden battlefield death of President Idriss Déby in April 2021 triggered an immediate military takeover in Chad, where a junta led by his son bypassed constitutional succession to seize control.

Idriss Déby had ruled Chad for three decades after seizing power in 1990. His tenure was marked by autocratic control, frequent rebel incursions, and a 2021 election widely viewed as fraudulent. Despite domestic repression, he remained a key Western ally in regional counterterrorism efforts.

On April 20, 2021, the Chadian army announced that President Idriss Déby had died from injuries sustained while visiting front lines during fighting against FACT rebels from Libya. Under the constitution, power should have transferred to the President of the National Assembly. Instead, a group of fifteen army generals dissolved the government and parliament and formed a Transitional Military Council (CMT). This junta installed the president’s son, General Mahamat Idriss Déby, as the new leader. The military immediately suspended the constitution, closed borders, and imposed a nationwide curfew to consolidate its authority. International allies such as France endorsed the new leadership despite its unconstitutional nature, citing the need for regional stability.

The takeover led to a prolonged transitional period initially set for eighteen months and later extended. The CMT faced domestic protests that were met with violent repression and arrests. Politically, the event ensured the Déby family maintained its grip on power despite promises of a return to civilian rule.
references: Harding, Andrew. "Chad's President Idriss Déby Dies After Clashes with Rebels." BBC News, April 20, 2021.
International Crisis Group. "Getting Chad's Transition on Track." September 30, 2021.
Institute for Security Studies. "Inconsistencies Cost the AU in Chad." June 20, 2023.
Rosenhart, Eline. "The Death of Chad's Warrior President." Ifriqiya, May 11, 2021.
Tubiana, Jérôme. "Le Tchad sous et après Déby." Politique africaine, 2021.
U.S. Department of State. "2021 Country Reports on Human Rights Practices: Chad." 2022.

title: Myanmar 2021 Coup Attempt
content: On February 1, 2021, Myanmar's military, known as the Tatmadaw, staged a coup that detained State Counsellor Aung San Suu Kyi, President Win Myint, and numerous other civilian leaders, ending the country's fragile democratic transition and returning Myanmar to military rule.

Myanmar's tentative democracy emerged after decades of military rule, with elections in 2015 bringing Aung San Suu Kyi's National League for Democracy (NLD) to power. Although constitutional restrictions prevented Suu Kyi from serving as president, she held significant authority as State Counsellor. However, the 2008 military-drafted constitution preserved military influence by reserving parliamentary seats and granting veto power over amendments. Tensions escalated after the November 2020 elections delivered a landslide victory to the NLD, while the military-aligned Union Solidarity and Development Party (USDP) performed poorly. The military alleged widespread electoral fraud without credible evidence.

In early morning raids on February 1, military forces arrested Suu Kyi, Win Myint, NLD lawmakers, cabinet members, and civil society activists across the country. The military shut down internet and communications and declared a one-year state of emergency under constitutional provisions. Vice President Myint Swe, a military appointee, became acting president and transferred authority to Commander-in-Chief Min Aung Hlaing. The military promised new elections, though analysts widely viewed the coup as preemptive and unconstitutional.

International condemnation was swift and widespread. Governments including the United States, United Kingdom, France, Australia, and the European Union denounced the coup. Myanmar’s ambassador to the United Nations, Kyaw Moe Tun, publicly opposed the military before being dismissed. Massive pro-democracy protests erupted nationwide, and security forces responded with lethal force, leading to a prolonged period of repression and instability that continues today.

title: Turkey 2016 Coup Attempt
content: On July 15, 2016, several thousand members of the Turkish military launched a coup attempt against President Recep Tayyip Erdogan.
Prime Minister Binali Yildirim announced that elements of the military were unlawfully attempting to seize power after reports of unauthorized military aircraft over Ankara.

Coup forces moved quickly to seize key sites across the country, including the Bosphorus Bridge, Taksim Square, and major television and radio stations. They also detained officials and reportedly forced General Hulusi Akar to sign a coup declaration.

State broadcasters were compelled to air a message from the conspirators, who identified themselves as the Peace at Home Council and claimed they were intervening because of what they described as the deterioration of secular and democratic principles in Turkey.

At the start of the coup attempt, President Erdogan was in Marmaris. After learning of the events, he flew to Istanbul and publicly denounced the coup. During the night, helicopters bombed state buildings, including the Turkish Parliament, and coup forces attempted to locate and detain Erdogan.

Despite early gains, the coup rapidly collapsed as loyalist forces regained control of major sites. By the morning of July 16, the Turkish government had largely restored order, and officials announced that the coup attempt had failed.

In the aftermath, Erdogan declared a state of emergency and launched a sweeping purge across the military, judiciary, education system, and wider public sector. Thousands of soldiers, judges, educators, and civilians were detained, suspended, or dismissed.

title: Egypt 2013 Coup Attempt
content: In 2011, mass protests during the Arab Spring forced President Hosni Mubarak to resign, transferring authority to the Supreme Council of the Armed Forces (SCAF).
After a transitional period under military oversight, Egypt held its first democratic elections. Mohamed Morsi of the Muslim Brotherhood’s Freedom and Justice Party became president on June 30, 2012.

Once in office, Morsi enacted sweeping reforms, including moves to reestablish parliament, redraw the constitution, and weaken SCAF’s authority. He also reshaped the government and military, consolidating presidential power.

In November 2012, Morsi issued a declaration expanding his powers and shielding his decisions from legal challenge. The move was widely criticized as authoritarian and triggered mass protests across Egypt.

Although Morsi later tried to calm tensions by describing the decree as temporary, opposition to his rule continued to grow. By June 2013, organizations such as Tamarod were mobilizing large demonstrations and claiming millions of signatures calling for his removal.

On June 30, 2013, millions of Egyptians protested against Morsi. On July 1, the military issued a 48-hour ultimatum demanding that the political crisis be resolved. As violence escalated and officials resigned, the military threatened to suspend the constitution.
After Morsi rejected the ultimatum, General Abdel Fattah el-Sisi ordered his arrest on July 3, 2013, announced a political transition, and installed Adly Mansour as interim president.

Following Morsi’s removal, the military suspended the constitution and violence continued. Security crackdowns on pro-Morsi demonstrations in July and August 2013 caused mass casualties, including the deadly dispersal of protest camps in Cairo.

In 2014, el-Sisi ran for president and won by a wide margin. Although elections formally restored civilian rule, the process took place under heavy repression and is widely viewed as marking the consolidation of a new authoritarian regime rather than a democratic restoration.

title: Pakistan 1999 Coup Attempt
content: On October 12, 1999, General Pervez Musharraf led a military coup against Prime Minister Nawaz Sharif.
While Musharraf was abroad, Sharif attempted to remove him as army chief. Military units loyal to Musharraf responded by securing the airport and preventing Sharif’s replacement from taking control.
The military then moved against Sharif’s government, arrested him, and took control of key government sites in Islamabad. Sharif offered no armed resistance and was removed from office.
In the days that followed, the military declared a state of emergency, suspended the constitution through executive orders, and dissolved parliament and provincial assemblies.

Pakistan’s political system had long been shaped by military intervention, and tensions between civilian leaders and the armed forces had intensified throughout the 1990s. Sharif’s efforts to assert civilian control over the military increased conflict with Musharraf and military elites.
The country also faced economic strain, political dysfunction, and institutional instability, all of which made military intervention easier to justify in the name of restoring order.
After the coup, Musharraf established military rule under a Chief Executive system, appointed a military-backed cabinet, and promised an eventual return to democratic governance.
The Supreme Court later validated the coup under the doctrine of necessity. Sharif was tried and ultimately exiled, while Musharraf remained in power from 1999 to 2008.
Although elections were later held, military dominance over Pakistan’s political system continued, and the coup significantly strengthened the armed forces’ role in governance.

title: Guinea-Bissau 1998 Coup Attempt
content: On June 7, 1998, General Ansumane Mané led a military mutiny and coup attempt against President João Bernardo Vieira in Guinea-Bissau.

Mané, a senior officer and former rebel commander, mobilized loyal troops and seized control of military barracks in Bissau. Rather than ending quickly, the coup attempt escalated into a prolonged civil war.

Guinea-Bissau had faced political instability and military factionalism since its 1994 transition to multiparty democracy. President Vieira, who had originally taken power in a 1980 coup, faced growing tensions with ambitious officers and discontent within the armed forces.

Mané believed Vieira was preparing to sideline him, while broader frustrations over corruption, military favoritism, and Vieira’s regional alliances with Senegal and Guinea deepened resentment inside the military.

Following the June 7 uprising, fighting spread rapidly through Bissau and surrounding areas. President Vieira called on Senegal and Guinea for military assistance, and both countries intervened in support of his government.

Although Mané’s forces did not achieve an immediate victory, they captured significant portions of the armed forces and large areas of national territory, while Vieira retained the capital with foreign military backing.
The conflict evolved into a devastating civil war lasting from June 1998 to May 1999, killing thousands and displacing large numbers of civilians.

The instability ultimately contributed to Vieira’s overthrow in May 1999. Mané himself was later killed in 2000 during further military clashes, and Guinea-Bissau continued to experience political instability and future coup activity.

title: Zambia 1997 Coup Attempt
content: On October 28, 1997, a small group of Zambian military officers and soldiers led by Captain Steven Lungu, also known as Captain Solo, attempted to overthrow President Frederick Chiluba.
The coup plotters seized the Zambia National Broadcasting Corporation and announced the coup, calling for Chiluba’s removal from office.

By the late 1990s, Chiluba’s government faced criticism over economic difficulties, governance problems, and military neglect. Zambia’s armed forces were frustrated by poor funding, inadequate conditions, and broader dissatisfaction with civilian leadership.

Political tensions had also increased after Chiluba backed constitutional changes that would have blocked former president Kenneth Kaunda from running again, raising concerns about democratic backsliding.

Despite dramatic early actions, the coup attempt failed to gain support from senior officers or broader factions within the military. Loyalist forces and security services moved rapidly to contain the rebellion.
Within hours, government forces surrounded the broadcaster, arrested the coup plotters, and restored control. The uprising collapsed almost immediately.

Captain Lungu and his associates were later tried and convicted of treason. The failed coup had little direct effect on Chiluba’s hold on power, but it exposed underlying military discontent and fragile civil-military relations in Zambia.
The event showed that while junior officers could still attempt to challenge the government, a coup without senior backing or wider military coordination had little chance of success.

title: Cambodia 1997 Coup Attempt
content: On July 5, 1997, Hun Sen used military forces loyal to him to remove his co-prime minister, Prince Norodom Ranariddh, from power in Cambodia.
International observers, including the United Nations, widely described the event as a coup that ended the fragile 1993 power-sharing arrangement created after UN-supervised elections.

Cambodia’s post-1993 political system was built on an unstable agreement between Ranariddh’s royalist FUNCINPEC and Hun Sen’s Cambodian People’s Party (CPP). Both leaders retained control over separate military factions and competed for supremacy within the state.
By 1996 and 1997, tensions had escalated as Hun Sen consolidated support among senior military officers and security forces and moved toward exclusive control.

On July 5, Hun Sen’s forces launched coordinated attacks on military positions, government buildings, and sites linked to Ranariddh’s supporters. Ranariddh, who was abroad at the time, could not organize an effective response.
Military units loyal to Ranariddh were defeated or switched allegiance to Hun Sen, allowing Hun Sen to take control of Phnom Penh and key state institutions within hours.

Hun Sen justified the takeover as necessary for political stability and more effective prosecution of the war against remaining Khmer Rouge forces. In practice, the coup eliminated his main political rival and ended the co-governing arrangement.

Afterward, Hun Sen consolidated exclusive power, elections were later held under his control in 1998, and Cambodia’s democratic transition was widely viewed as having been reversed in favor of long-term authoritarian rule.

title: Sierra Leone 1997 Coup Attempt
content: On May 25, 1997, junior soldiers and military personnel overthrew President Ahmad Tejan Kabbah’s civilian government in Sierra Leone.
The coup took place less than two months after Kabbah had taken office following the March 1996 democratic elections, reversing Sierra Leone’s fragile transition to civilian rule.

Sierra Leone had previously been ruled by the military-led National Provisional Ruling Council (NPRC) since 1992. Although elections in 1996 restored civilian government, the country remained deeply unstable because of the ongoing civil war with the Revolutionary United Front (RUF).

Many military officers resented civilian control and believed Kabbah’s government lacked the resources and authority to effectively manage the war. Military dissatisfaction over pay, conditions, and autonomy also intensified.

On May 25, mutinous soldiers seized installations and government buildings in Freetown. President Kabbah fled the country, and the coup plotters freed Johnny Paul Koroma from prison, elevating him as leader of the new Armed Forces Revolutionary Council (AFRC).

The AFRC junta took power and aligned itself with the broader military struggle during the civil war. The coup was executed quickly, and the civilian government was unable to mount an organized resistance.

The junta’s hold on power was short-lived. The Economic Community of West African States Monitoring Group (ECOMOG), led by Nigeria and supported by regional allies, intervened militarily to restore Kabbah.

In February 1998, ECOMOG forces pushed the AFRC and its allies out of Freetown, allowing Kabbah to return to office. The episode highlighted the fragility of Sierra Leone’s democratic transition and the powerful role of the military during wartime.

title: Bangladesh 1996 Coup Attempt
content: On May 20, 1996, Lieutenant General Abu Saleh Mohammad Nasim, then Bangladesh’s Army Chief of Staff, challenged civilian presidential authority in a confrontation that raised fears of a military coup.

Bangladesh had experienced repeated coups and military-dominated governments since independence, and by 1996 it was still navigating a fragile return to democratic rule under a caretaker government system.

President Abdur Rahman Biswas ordered the dismissal of two senior military officers who were allegedly involved in a conspiracy. Nasim refused to carry out the presidential order and instead moved toward direct confrontation with civilian authority.

Nasim regarded the dismissals as threatening to military institutional interests and his own standing within the armed forces. The standoff reflected deeper tensions between military autonomy and attempts to strengthen civilian control.
President Biswas responded by dismissing Nasim himself from his post as army chief. Nasim was subsequently arrested and subjected to legal proceedings.

Unlike many earlier South Asian military interventions, the confrontation did not escalate into widespread armed conflict. Civilian constitutional authority prevailed through institutional action rather than military force.
The incident strengthened the caretaker government ahead of parliamentary elections held in June 1996, which helped restore competitive multiparty politics.

Although the military remained influential, the episode was symbolically important because it showed that civilian institutions in Bangladesh could resist and defeat a direct challenge from the armed forces.

title: Guinea 1996 Coup Attempt
content: On February 2–3, 1996, approximately 2,000 soldiers launched a military uprising against President Lansana Conté in Guinea.

The unrest was driven largely by grievances over pay, living conditions, and the distribution of resources within the military, though it also took on broader political dimensions that made it resemble a coup attempt.
Guinea had been moving uneasily toward multiparty democracy since the early 1990s, but President Conté remained the dominant political figure and democratic institutions were weak.
Economic hardship, poor military funding, and the broader regional pattern of instability in West Africa increased tensions between the armed forces and the civilian-led state.

Beginning on February 2, soldiers seized barracks and military installations in Conakry and nearby areas. While the movement was rooted in material grievances, some participants also raised wider demands that challenged the authority of Conté’s government.
Conté responded by combining negotiations with military pressure. Loyalist forces were mobilized, while the government also made concessions related to military conditions.
The uprising lacked the senior leadership and sustained organization necessary to completely topple the government, and the authorities were eventually able to suppress it.
Although Conté survived, the event exposed major weaknesses in Guinea’s civil-military relations and showed how military grievances could evolve into a direct political threat against the state.

title: Niger 1996 Coup Attempt
content: On January 27, 1996, Colonel Ibrahim Baré Maïnassara led a bloodless military coup against President Mahamane Ousmane in Niger.
The coup overthrew Niger’s first democratically elected president and marked the collapse of the country’s early post-transition experiment with civilian multiparty rule.

Niger had adopted democratic institutions after a 1991 national conference and the 1992 elections, but the new system was weakened by economic crisis, institutional rivalry, and tension between civilian leaders and the military.

President Ousmane and Prime Minister Hama Amadou were locked in political conflict under Niger’s semi-presidential system, while the armed forces increasingly viewed civilian governance as ineffective and threatening to military autonomy.

On January 27, Maïnassara and allied officers seized government buildings, the presidential palace, and other key sites in Niamey. Ousmane and Amadou were arrested, and the military quickly consolidated control.
Maïnassara declared a state of emergency, suspended the constitution, dissolved parliament, and announced the formation of the National Supreme Council for the Orientation of the State (CNSOE).

Although the junta promised a return to civilian rule, elections held under military oversight in 1996 and 1997 enabled Maïnassara to consolidate personal power while preserving only the appearance of democratic governance.
The coup exposed the weakness of Niger’s democratic institutions and reinforced military dominance in politics until Maïnassara’s assassination in 1999 triggered further instability.

title: Sierra Leone 1995 Coup Attempt
content: On October 3, 1995, Sierra Leone’s military government announced that it had uncovered a failed coup plot involving several senior officers opposed to the country’s planned return to civilian rule.

The alleged conspiracy emerged during the rule of the National Provisional Ruling Council (NPRC), the military junta that had governed Sierra Leone since the 1992 coup led by Captain Valentine Strasser.

By 1995, the NPRC had committed to holding multiparty elections in early 1996, creating internal divisions within the military as some officers feared losing power, influence, and access to state resources under democratic rule.

According to the government, six officers ranging in rank from lieutenant to major were arrested, while two additional lieutenants remained at large. The exact scope of the conspiracy has been disputed in later accounts.

Some reports described the event as a coup attempt that was foiled before it could fully unfold, while others suggested it was more limited and may have involved less coordination than the government claimed.
The plot did not prevent Sierra Leone’s transition. Elections went ahead in March 1996, and Ahmad Tejan Kabbah was elected president, restoring civilian rule.
Even so, the episode demonstrated continued military resistance to democratization and highlighted the fragile nature of Sierra Leone’s political transition amid the ongoing civil war.
That instability persisted, and in May 1997 disaffected soldiers would carry out a more substantial coup that overthrew Kabbah’s government.

title: Sao Tome and Principe 1995 Coup Attempt
content: On August 15, 1995, Second Lieutenants Manuel Quintas de Almeida and Fernando Sousa Pontes led the first military coup in the history of Sao Tome and Principe, overthrowing President Miguel Trovoada’s civilian government.

The coup was driven largely by severe military neglect. The armed forces faced months of unpaid wages, extreme underfunding, shortages of uniforms and equipment, and poor living conditions, creating deep resentment among junior officers and recruits.

Around 40 soldiers left their barracks in the early morning and seized the Presidential Palace in Sao Tome. President Trovoada and Prime Minister Carlos Graça were detained, and Almeida declared himself chairman of a Junta of National Salvation.

The coup leaders claimed the military would govern the country, but their hold on power was immediately challenged by strong international opposition. Angola, France, Portugal, and the United Nations all pressured the junta to restore civilian rule.

Under this pressure, the military quickly softened its position and proposed a government of national unity. Trovoada refused to participate in any military-led arrangement, and opposition parties also rejected the junta’s compromise proposals.

Angola ultimately mediated negotiations between the military and civilian leaders. On August 22, 1995, both sides signed a memorandum of understanding and the armed forces agreed to return power to the civilian government.
In exchange, the military received concessions including promises of armed forces restructuring, salary increases, promotions, and greater influence in the selection of the defense minister.

Although constitutional order was restored within a week, the coup exposed deep tensions between civilian leaders and the military, tensions that would reappear in later years, including another coup attempt in 2003.

title: Qatar 1995 Coup Attempt
content: On June 27, 1995, Sheikh Hamad bin Khalifa Al Thani carried out a bloodless palace coup against his father, Emir Sheikh Khalifa bin Hamad Al Thani, and took power in Qatar.
The coup took place while Sheikh Khalifa was abroad in Switzerland. Hamad coordinated with military and security forces loyal to him and quickly secured control of key state institutions.

Qatar in the mid-1990s was ruled by an aging emir whose leadership faced criticism from younger reform-minded royals and technocrats. Many believed the country needed modernization, stronger institutions, and a more ambitious foreign policy.

Hamad, who had military training and favored reform, viewed his father’s rule as outdated and believed Qatar needed economic diversification, international engagement, and more assertive regional influence.

On the day of the coup, forces aligned with Hamad took over government buildings, state media, and strategic ministries. The transfer of power happened without major violence or resistance, largely because the security services backed Hamad.

Hamad then declared himself emir and presented the takeover as necessary for Qatar’s modernization and development. Unlike many coups, this was not a military overthrow of a democratic government but an intra-dynastic succession struggle resolved through control of state institutions.

After taking power, Hamad launched major reforms. Under his rule, Qatar pursued economic diversification, more independent regional diplomacy, close ties with the United States, and the establishment of Al Jazeera in 1996.

A countercoup attempt by supporters of the former emir in 1996 failed, further strengthening Hamad’s rule. The 1995 coup ultimately reshaped Qatar’s political and international trajectory while preserving monarchical continuity.

title: Iraq 1995 Coup Attempt
content: On June 15–16, 1995, Brigadier Turki Ismael al-Dulaimi led a failed coup attempt against Iraqi President Saddam Hussein.

Dulaimi commanded an armored brigade from the elite July 14 Brigade, named after the date of Iraq’s 1958 revolution. His forces seized the Abu Ghraib broadcast station west of Baghdad in an effort to announce an anti-Saddam government and attract wider military support.

The coup took place during a period of severe strain in Iraq. Following the 1991 Gulf War, the country was suffering under sweeping international sanctions, economic collapse, and deep military dissatisfaction with Saddam’s rule.

Senior officers and Republican Guard elements were increasingly discontented by dwindling resources, worsening living conditions, and Saddam’s repressive, highly personalized control of the state. Elements of the Dulaimi tribe, once important to the regime, had also begun turning against him.

Despite the initial seizure of the broadcast station, two brigades from the same military base remained loyal to Saddam and moved rapidly to crush the rebellion. Government tanks and helicopter gunships joined the counterattack.
The fighting lasted for hours, and the coup collapsed before it could gain momentum. Facing defeat, Dulaimi committed suicide.

After the attempted coup, Saddam intensified his repression of the armed forces. In the following year, hundreds of military officers were reportedly executed in connection with coup plotting and opposition activity, reinforcing the regime’s climate of fear.
The failed uprising showed that discontent within Iraq’s military remained serious, but also demonstrated Saddam’s ability to suppress threats through overwhelming force, surveillance, and purges.

title: Azerbaijan 1995 Coup Attempt
content: From March 13 to March 17, 1995, Colonel Rovshan Javadov led a mutiny by approximately 700 members of Azerbaijan’s Special Purpose Police Unit (OPON) against President Heydar Aliyev.
Javadov, who served as deputy interior minister and commanded the powerful OPON force, had developed significant autonomous influence within Azerbaijan’s post-Soviet security structure.

Azerbaijan in the mid-1990s was marked by military factionalism, weak institutions, and ongoing struggles for control after independence. Tensions between Aliyev and Javadov had already surfaced in 1994, when OPON defied presidential orders and signaled its willingness to challenge the state.

By early 1995, Javadov and supporters of former president Abulfaz Elchibey were accused of plotting to remove Aliyev and restore Elchibey, with alleged backing from nationalist elements inside Turkey’s security establishment.

The mutiny escalated after the interception of a copper shipment and the barricading of OPON forces in their headquarters in Baku. Turkish intelligence reportedly warned Aliyev in advance that a coup attempt was being planned.
Aliyev responded by mobilizing loyal government forces. On March 17, after a prolonged firefight, state forces stormed the OPON compound, killing Javadov and crushing the uprising.

Official reports placed the death toll at around 40, though unofficial estimates were higher. In the aftermath, Aliyev further consolidated power and used the failed mutiny to justify crackdowns on opposition figures and military rivals.

Later trials of the accused coup plotters were marred by allegations of torture and forced confessions. The event highlighted the fragility of Aliyev’s early rule and the persistence of armed opposition within Azerbaijan’s security institutions.

title: Gambia 1994 Coup Attempt
content: On November 11, 1994, Lieutenant Basiru Barrow led a counter-coup attempt against Yahya Jammeh’s newly established military regime in The Gambia.
The attempt came just four months after Jammeh’s own July 1994 coup and reflected deep internal fractures within the Armed Forces Provisional Ruling Council (AFPRC).

The July coup had been described as loosely organized and initially based on informal promises among the junior officers involved, including better pay, improved military welfare, and a short transition back to barracks after an interim period.

Instead, Jammeh quickly consolidated personal control and abandoned many of those understandings. This generated growing resentment among soldiers and junior officers who felt betrayed by his monopolization of power.

On November 11, Barrow and allied soldiers moved against Jammeh’s faction at Yundum military camp, the country’s largest military installation. Fighting broke out as Barrow’s group attempted to challenge Jammeh’s hold on the junta.
Jammeh’s forces proved stronger and better organized. Barrow was killed during the clashes, and the counter-coup collapsed.

After suppressing the rebellion, Jammeh’s faction reportedly carried out a purge. Testimony later presented to The Gambia’s Truth, Reconciliation and Reparation Commission alleged that nearly two dozen soldiers linked to the counter-coup were executed, although the exact number remains contested.
The failed November 1994 counter-coup helped cement Jammeh’s authority over the military and foreshadowed the authoritarian rule he would maintain for more than two decades.

title: Azerbaijan 1994 Coup Attempt
content: On October 4, 1994, Azerbaijan experienced a military uprising and attempted coup centered in the city of Ganja, with Prime Minister Surat Huseynov accused of involvement.
The event unfolded during a period of severe instability after the Nagorno-Karabakh war and the 1993 political transition that had brought Heydar Aliyev to power.

Huseynov had been a major military figure in the earlier struggle for power and retained significant influence within Azerbaijan’s armed forces. By late 1994, tensions between Aliyev and Huseynov had deepened as Aliyev moved to consolidate authority.
The Azerbaijani government claimed that Huseynov, allegedly with Russian backing, was trying to overthrow Aliyev and restore pro-Russian influence in Azerbaijani politics.
The uprising was centered in Ganja, Azerbaijan’s second-largest city. In response, the government declared a state of emergency and mobilized loyalist forces to suppress the challenge.
Although the precise scale of the incident remains disputed, the government treated it as a serious coup attempt and used it to remove Huseynov from office.
Huseynov fled to Russia, where he received asylum despite Azerbaijani demands for extradition. His escape strengthened Aliyev’s claims that Russian actors had supported the plot.

The failed uprising exposed continuing military factionalism and showed how fragile Aliyev’s hold on power remained in the early years of his presidency. Huseynov was eventually extradited back to Azerbaijan in 1997 and later sentenced to life imprisonment.

title: Liberia 1994 Coup Attempt
content: On September 15, 1994, General Charles Julu led a coup attempt against Liberia’s transitional government during the First Liberian Civil War.

Julu commanded roughly 100 soldiers from the Armed Forces of Liberia and launched an assault on the Executive Mansion in Monrovia, hoping to seize power as the transitional government’s mandate neared expiration.
Liberia at the time was deeply fragmented by civil war. Multiple armed factions controlled different parts of the country, while ECOMOG peacekeepers had become the main security force in Monrovia.

The transitional government, headed by President Amos Sawyer, faced political uncertainty because its mandate was close to expiring, creating fears of a power vacuum that Julu later claimed had motivated his intervention.

On September 15, Julu’s forces occupied the Executive Mansion and attempted to displace the interim authorities. ECOMOG reacted immediately, launching an assault from both land and sea, including naval gunfire from offshore.
After intense but short fighting, ECOMOG stormed the Executive Mansion and defeated the coup forces within hours. Julu and around 80 others were arrested.

The attempted coup had little lasting effect on the structure of Liberia’s transitional government, but it highlighted how fragile the wartime institutions remained and how dependent the state was on ECOMOG for survival.

Julu was convicted of sedition and sentenced to life imprisonment, though he was later released in 1996. The episode showed both the volatility of Liberia’s civil war politics and the decisive role of regional peacekeepers in containing military takeovers.

title: Lesotho 1994 Coup Attempt
content: On August 17, 1994, King Letsie III carried out a palace coup against Lesotho’s democratically elected government led by Prime Minister Ntsu Mokhehle and the Basutoland Congress Party (BCP).

The coup took place only a year after Lesotho’s 1993 democratic elections, which had ended more than two decades of military and authoritarian rule and given the BCP overwhelming control of parliament.

Despite the election result, the new civilian government faced resistance from opposition groups, parts of the security establishment, and the monarchy. Opposition parties claimed fraud, while the Lesotho Defense Force retained political ambitions and influence.
King Letsie III, formally a constitutional monarch, had his own political ambitions and was also tied to disputes involving the exile of his father, King Moshoeshoe II.

Using a petition from opposition parties as justification, Letsie suspended sections of the constitution, dissolved parliament, dismissed Mokhehle’s government, and appointed a provisional ruling council.

The move sparked immediate public resistance. On August 18, thousands of BCP supporters protested in Maseru, and clashes with security forces left at least four people dead and several others injured.
The coup quickly faced strong domestic opposition as well as international pressure from neighboring states, the Organization of African Unity, and other external actors.

Under that pressure, Letsie reversed the coup on September 14, 1994, and restored the elected government. The episode exposed the fragility of Lesotho’s democracy and the continuing instability caused by tensions among the monarchy, military, and civilian institutions.

title: Gambia 1994 Coup Attempt
content: On July 22–23, 1994, junior military officers led by Lieutenant Yahya Jammeh overthrew President Dawda Jawara in a bloodless coup, ending one of Africa’s longest-standing democratic governments.
Jawara had governed The Gambia since it became a republic in 1970, but by the mid-1990s his administration faced growing criticism for corruption, economic weakness, and governmental inertia.

Discontent was particularly strong among younger Gambians and within the military. Junior officers complained about delayed salaries, poor advancement opportunities, and resentment toward senior Nigerian military advisers.

The coup appears to have emerged more as a spontaneous mutiny than a highly planned operation. Overnight, a group of junior soldiers agreed to move against the government, and by the next morning about 200 soldiers had joined the effort.
President Jawara fled to the USS La Moure County offshore and sought outside assistance, but neither the United States nor Senegal intervened to restore him to power.

Within hours, Jammeh and fellow officers had seized control and established the Armed Forces Provisional Ruling Council (AFPRC). They abolished the constitution, arrested officials, and dissolved the existing government.

Although Jammeh initially promised a short military transition followed by a return to civilian rule, he instead consolidated power, later winning tightly controlled elections and ruling The Gambia for 22 years.

The 1994 coup ended the country’s First Republic and marked the beginning of a long period of authoritarian military-dominated rule associated with repression, human rights abuses, and the dismantling of democratic institutions.

title: Rwanda 1994 Coup Attempt
content: On April 6, 1994, the plane carrying Rwandan President Juvénal Habyarimana and Burundian President Cyprien Ntaryamira was shot down as it approached Kigali, killing everyone on board.

Within hours, on April 7, organized killings began across Rwanda. Members of the Presidential Guard, government forces, and militia groups launched systematic attacks against Tutsi civilians and moderate Hutu political leaders.

The assassination served as the trigger for genocide rather than a traditional coup, as extremist Hutu factions had already prepared for mass violence through weapons stockpiling, propaganda campaigns, and coordinated planning.
Rwanda had been deeply divided along ethnic lines, and tensions had intensified during the 1990–1994 civil war between the Hutu-led government and the Tutsi-led Rwandan Patriotic Front (RPF).
The Arusha Accords of 1993 attempted to establish power-sharing, but extremist Hutu factions strongly opposed them and viewed them as a threat to their dominance.

Immediately after the plane crash, roadblocks were established across Kigali, and targeted killings began. Prime Minister Agathe Uwilingiyimana and UN peacekeepers assigned to protect her were among the first victims.
Over approximately 100 days, an estimated 800,000 to 1.1 million people were killed, primarily Tutsi civilians but also moderate Hutus who opposed the violence.

The genocide ended in July 1994 when the RPF captured Kigali and defeated the genocidal regime, leading to massive refugee flows and the establishment of international tribunals to prosecute those responsible.

title: Nigeria 1993 Coup Attempt
content: On November 17, 1993, General Sani Abacha carried out a bloodless military coup, forcing Interim President Ernest Shonekan to resign and assuming control of Nigeria.
The coup followed months of political crisis triggered by the annulment of the June 12, 1993 presidential election, widely considered the fairest in Nigerian history.
After annulling the election, General Ibrahim Babangida installed Shonekan as interim president in August 1993, but his government lacked legitimacy and struggled to restore stability.
By November, the military had grown dissatisfied with the civilian-led transition, and Abacha, who served as Defence Minister, held decisive influence over the armed forces.
Abacha forced Shonekan’s resignation without significant resistance and quickly consolidated control over state institutions.
In a national broadcast, he justified the takeover by citing governmental stagnation and failure to manage the transition to democracy.
After taking power, Abacha established the Provisional Ruling Council and ruled Nigeria through military decrees, suspending democratic processes and restricting civil liberties.

His regime became increasingly authoritarian, marked by repression, political imprisonment, and human rights abuses, and lasted until his death in 1998, after which Nigeria transitioned back to civilian rule.

title: Libya 1993 Coup Attempt
content: On October 23, 1993, officers from Libya’s Warfallah tribe attempted a military coup against Colonel Muammar Gaddafi’s regime.
The uprising reflected growing dissatisfaction within segments of the military over political marginalization and unequal distribution of resources.
Libya at the time faced international sanctions and economic hardship following the 1988 Lockerbie bombing, which intensified internal tensions.
The Warfallah tribe, one of Libya’s largest and historically influential groups, believed it had been sidelined in favor of other loyalist factions within Gaddafi’s regime.
Military units aligned with the Warfallah officers initiated the coup attempt, challenging central authority and attempting to overthrow the government.
Gaddafi’s security forces and loyal military units responded quickly, suppressing the uprising through superior organization and force.
The coup failed within a short period, and many of the officers involved were arrested, executed, or imprisoned.
The failed attempt demonstrated ongoing tribal and military tensions within Libya and reinforced Gaddafi’s reliance on repression to maintain power until his eventual overthrow in 2011.

title: Burundi 1993 Coup Attempt
content: On October 21, 1993, elements of Burundi’s Tutsi-dominated military carried out a coup attempt and assassinated President Melchior Ndadaye, the country’s first democratically elected and first Hutu president.
Ndadaye had been in office for only three months and had begun implementing reforms aimed at reducing Tutsi dominance within the military and government.
These reforms alarmed military officers and political elites who feared loss of power, leading to a coordinated effort to remove him.
Military units seized control of the presidential palace and detained Ndadaye and other officials under the pretense of protecting them.
Ndadaye was subsequently killed at a military base, along with other members of his government.
Although coup leaders attempted to establish a new government, strong domestic resistance and international pressure prevented full consolidation of power.
The assassination triggered widespread ethnic violence between Hutu and Tutsi populations, resulting in tens of thousands of deaths within months.
The crisis escalated into a prolonged civil war that lasted until 2005, causing hundreds of thousands of casualties and long-term political instability.

title: Burundi 1993 Coup Attempt
content: On July 3, 1993, soldiers from Burundi’s Second Commando Battalion launched a failed coup attempt shortly before the inauguration of President-elect Melchior Ndadaye.
The coup occurred during a critical transition period following democratic elections that brought Ndadaye, a Hutu leader, to power for the first time.
The military, historically dominated by Tutsi officers, viewed the shift toward Hutu political leadership as a threat to its institutional power.
Mutineering soldiers attempted to disrupt the transition and prevent Ndadaye from taking office.
The coup attempt was suppressed by forces loyal to the transitional government before it could succeed.
Ndadaye was successfully sworn in as president on July 10, 1993, marking a major milestone in Burundi’s democratic transition.
However, the failed July coup revealed deep divisions within the military and foreshadowed further instability.
Just months later, in October 1993, a more successful coup attempt led to Ndadaye’s assassination and triggered a civil war.

title: Venezuela 1992 Coup Attempt
content: On November 27, 1992, Venezuelan military forces led by Admiral Carlos Molina Tamayo launched a second coup attempt against President Carlos Andrés Pérez.
The uprising followed an earlier failed coup attempt in February 1992 led by Hugo Chávez, reflecting ongoing military dissatisfaction with the government.
Venezuela was experiencing severe economic crisis and political unrest, driven by unpopular economic reforms and declining living conditions.
Naval and air force units participated in the November coup, attempting coordinated attacks against the government.
Government forces responded quickly, suppressing the uprising within approximately 24 hours.
The coup failed to gain widespread military support, which limited its ability to challenge the regime effectively.
Although unsuccessful, the repeated coup attempts demonstrated deep institutional fractures within Venezuela’s military and political system.
The instability contributed to Pérez’s eventual removal from office in 1993 and the long-term political transformation that followed.
references: Wikipedia. "November 1992 Venezuelan Coup Attempt."

title: Madagascar 1992 Coup Attempt
content: On July 29, 1992, Madagascar held a presidential runoff election that completed the transition from interim government to elected civilian rule.
Albert Zafy won the election, defeating his opponent and ending the interim administration led by Guy Razanamasy.
The election followed a period of political upheaval, including a 1991 popular uprising that forced longtime leader Didier Ratsiraka from power.
Madagascar had been moving toward multiparty democracy after years of authoritarian rule and international pressure for reform.
The election process was monitored by international observers and proceeded relatively peacefully compared to earlier instability.
Zafy’s victory represented a popular mandate for democratic governance and a break from military-dominated leadership.
Despite the successful transition, Zafy’s presidency faced economic challenges and political fragmentation.
He was eventually removed from office in 1997, illustrating the continued fragility of democratic institutions in Madagascar.

title: Sierra Leone 1992 Coup Attempt
content: On April 29, 1992, Captain Valentine Strasser led a military coup that overthrew President Joseph Saidu Momoh in Sierra Leone.
The coup was driven by frustration among junior officers over poor conditions, lack of resources, and ineffective leadership in the ongoing civil war against the Revolutionary United Front (RUF).
Strasser and approximately 60 soldiers marched from Kailahun to Freetown and seized control of the State House.
President Momoh fled the country and sought refuge in Guinea as the military took control of the government.
Strasser and his allies established the National Provisional Ruling Council (NPRC), with Strasser becoming the world’s youngest head of state at age 25.
The coup was largely bloodless and initially welcomed by many citizens who were dissatisfied with the government’s handling of the civil war.
The NPRC attempted to combat the RUF insurgency but struggled due to limited resources and ongoing instability.
Although elections were eventually held in 1996, Sierra Leone continued to experience coups and civil war throughout the decade.

title: Venezuela 1992 Coup Attempt
content: On February 4, 1992, Lieutenant Colonel Hugo Chávez led a failed military coup attempt against President Carlos Andrés Pérez in Venezuela.
The coup was motivated by widespread dissatisfaction with economic conditions and opposition to neoliberal reforms implemented under Pérez’s government.
Chávez and approximately 200 troops launched coordinated attacks on key government installations, including the presidential palace in Caracas.
Despite initial movements, the coup failed to gain support from senior military leadership, and loyal forces quickly suppressed the uprising.
Chávez surrendered within hours and was imprisoned following the failed attempt.
Although unsuccessful, the coup significantly raised Chávez’s national profile, particularly after his televised statement accepting responsibility.
Venezuela continued to experience political instability, including another coup attempt in November 1992 and corruption scandals.
Chávez was later pardoned in 1994 and eventually elected president in 1998, transforming Venezuelan politics.
